nativeRange.habitat: Silty or sandy lagoon and bay areas; inhabits burrows under rocks, typically in mated pairs

diet.notes: Naturally feeds by sifting sand through its gills to extract small crustaceans, mysis shrimp, copepods, amphipods, and other small invertebrates. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, live black worms, and prepared carnivore foods. Generally advised to offer varied meaty foods multiple times per day, as this species tends to have a high metabolism from its continuous sand-sifting behavior. A well-populated refugium or live sand bed with abundant microfauna is generally considered beneficial.

goodWith: other gobies of different body shape
avoidWith: aggressive tankmates that compete for bottom territory

summary: A sand-sifting saltwater goby from the Indo-Pacific, generally considered reef-safe and peaceful. Tends to form mated pairs, sifts live sand continuously, and is generally advised for tanks with a deep, live sand bed and a populated refugium.
careNotes:
Valenciennea sexguttata is a sand-sifting goby that spends most of its time on or near the substrate, filtering sand through its gills to extract small invertebrates. It tends to form monogamous mated pairs in the wild and in captivity, and generally fares better when kept as a bonded pair. Single specimens have been reported to do well but may be shyer.

A deep live-sand bed (ideally 7–10 cm or more) and an attached, well-populated refugium are generally considered important for long-term success, as the fish relies on a steady supply of small invertebrates and microfauna. Without adequate microfauna, the fish may slowly decline.

This species is a documented jumper; a tight-fitting, secure lid is advised. It tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and similar-shaped gobies — best kept singly or as a verified mated pair.

Minimum tank size showed notable disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria and Bay Bridge Aquarium recommend 30 gallons (~114 L), BlueZoo Aquatics recommends 50 gallons (~189 L), and ReefApp recommends 500 liters as a general guideline for the species' sand-sifting needs. The value of 189 L (50 gallons) used here represents a moderate consensus between the smaller hobby recommendations and the larger specialist recommendation; reviewers may wish to adjust based on the specific setup.

Temperature also showed slight disagreement: hobbyist sources (LiveAquaria, Bay Bridge) recommend 72–78°F (22–26°C), while FishBase wild data suggests 22–28°C. An averaged range of 22–27°C is used here.

Lifespan is reported as 3–5 years by most sources, though some accounts suggest longer lifespans of up to 10 years in well-maintained systems.
breedingNotes: Valenciennea sexguttata has been reported to spawn in captivity, though successful rearing of larvae is considered difficult. The species tends to form mated pairs and has been documented laying adhesive eggs in a burrow or under a rock. Parental care of the eggs has been observed. Larvae are planktonic and are generally considered challenging to raise in captivity due to their small size and specialized feeding requirements.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked common names and sizing. This source lists the species as 'White Sleeper Goby' and includes synonyms: Ghost Sleeper Goby, Ladder Glider Goby, Sixspot Sleeper Goby. Tank minimum 50 gallons.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Wild data: max TL 14 cm; native range Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/Persian Gulf to Samoa; temperature 22–28°C (preferred 26.9–29.3°C); depth 3–25 m; confirms taxonomy Gobiidae/Gobiiformes.

additional.name: Bay Bridge Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (5.5 in), care level (Easy — lower than LiveAquaria Moderate), temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, lifespan 3–5 years, tank 30 gallons. Care level averaged to intermediate between Easy and Moderate.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Recommends 500 L minimum tank (outlier vs 30–50 gal hobby consensus), likely reflecting sand-sifting footprint requirements. Lists reef safe with caution (minority view vs yes from other 3 sources). Feeding 4+ times daily recommended. Confirms peaceful but territorial toward conspecifics.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity, with pair bonding and burrow-based egg laying documented. Larval rearing is generally considered difficult due to the planktonic nature and small size of larvae.
